content:
The combined effect of temperature and humidity on adhesive joint integrity provides the central design constraint for DVS-PEI encapsulants operating in damp heat environments. Tensile strength of high-temperature curing epoxy adhesive joints decreased by approximately 31.3% after moisture absorption alone and by 27.7% under high temperature (95 degC) dry conditions [REF:1]. Under combined high temperature and humid conditions, the decrease reached 54.4% [REF:1] -- exceeding the sum of individual effects (31.3% + 27.7% = 59.0%, though the combined loss of 54.4% demonstrates that the two degradation pathways interact rather than acting independently). This synergistic degradation profile [REF:1] establishes that encapsulant adhesion design must account for the simultaneous presence of heat and moisture rather than treating each stressor in isolation.

Accelerated aging protocols provide a standardized framework for evaluating encapsulant formulations against this combined threat. PEVA films with four different VA content levels (18%, 24%, 33%, and 40%) were fabricated with necessary additives, cured at 150 degC under vacuum, and then subjected to accelerated damp heat aging at 85 degC and 85% relative humidity according to IEC 61215 for durations of 1,000 and 2,000 hours [REF:11]. This experimental design -- spanning a wide compositional range under standardized IEC conditions -- provides a methodological template applicable to DVS-PEI formulation screening [REF:11]. The IEC 61215 protocol (85 degC / 85% RH) [REF:11] approximates the combined thermal-humidity stress that flexible electronics encounter in tropical and subtropical deployment environments, making it a relevant accelerated test for DVS-PEI qualification.

Translating these findings to DVS-PEI design implies several actionable principles. First, the 54.4% tensile strength loss under combined conditions [REF:1] sets a minimum performance margin: any DVS-PEI adhesive system must retain sufficient interfacial strength after losing more than half its initial value to remain functional in service. Second, the demonstrated sensitivity of PEVA mechanical properties to compositional variation (VA content spanning 18-40%) [REF:11] indicates that analogous compositional optimization -- identifying the DVS-PEI monomer ratios, crosslink densities, and additive packages that maximize damp heat resistance -- is essential and cannot be assumed from thermal-only or moisture-only screening.

The evidence base for DVS-PEI-specific design criteria remains critically thin. The synergistic degradation data [REF:1] derive from epoxy adhesive joints, not polyetherimide-based systems, and the PEVA compositional study [REF:11] addresses a chemically distinct polymer class. Whether DVS-PEI encapsulants exhibit the same moisture-temperature synergy magnitude, the same compositional sensitivity, or the same failure modes (interfacial versus cohesive) as these reference systems is unknown. Target elastic modulus ranges, moisture barrier performance metrics, and CTE-mismatch thresholds specific to DVS-PEI on flexible substrates are entirely absent from the current literature. Systematic damp heat aging studies of DVS-PEI encapsulants -- using IEC 61215 protocols at multiple durations with peel force, fracture energy, and surface chemistry characterization -- represent the highest-priority research need identified by this review.

**Key Findings**

- Tensile strength of epoxy adhesive joints decreased by 31.3% (moisture alone), 27.7% (95 degC dry), and 54.4% (combined high temperature and humidity), demonstrating synergistic damp heat degradation [REF:1].
- PEVA films across four VA content levels (18-40%) were tested under IEC 61215 damp heat aging (85 degC / 85% RH) at 1,000 and 2,000 hours, providing a compositional screening template applicable to DVS-PEI [REF:11].
- Combined thermal-humidity exposure produces degradation exceeding the sum of individual stressors, requiring DVS-PEI design margins that account for synergistic loss [REF:1].
- DVS-PEI-specific damp heat aging data -- including adhesion retention, failure mode characterization, and compositional optimization -- represent the most critical evidence gap for flexible electronics encapsulant design.
